what: Comparing Methods Using a Shared Cross-Validation Split with tune_imp()
title: slideimp
text: Simulate data Instead random NA sampling inside tune_imp(), internally calls sample_na_loc(), can generate NA locations front pass tune_imp(). na_loc 5 elements, one repeat. element matrix whose rows store row column index missing value. can compare 1) PCA, 2) K-NN imputation, 3) custom method, since cross-validation missing values methods. Note: custom function requires first argument obj, must return object dimensions obj, subsequent arguments must match column names parameters data frame. Pick method lowest cross-validation error:
code: # 20 rows, 1000 columns, all columns have at least some NA sim_obj <- sim_mat(n = 20, p = 1000, perc_col_na = 1) obj <- sim_obj$input obj[1:4, 1:4] #>          feature1  feature2  feature3  feature4 #> sample1 0.3486482 0.7385414 0.4077444 0.1607935 #> sample2 0.5338935 0.4724364 0.9663621 0.3722729 #> sample3 0.7185848 0.7351035 0.6724479 0.3162537 #> sample4 0.1734418        NA 0.0000000 0.0000000 na_loc <- sample_na_loc(obj, n_cols = 200, n_rows = 5, n_reps = 5) length(na_loc) #> [1] 5 na_loc[[1]][1:6, ] #>      row col #> [1,]  18 661 #> [2,]  17 661 #> [3,]  20 661 #> [4,]  15 661 #> [5,]   6 661 #> [6,]  10 293 # This custom function imputes missing values with random normal values and takes # `mean` and `sd` as params rnorm_imp <- function(obj, mean, sd) {   na <- is.na(obj)   obj[na] <- rnorm(sum(na), mean = mean, sd = sd) # <- impute values with rnorm   return(obj) # <- return an imputed object with the same dim as obj }  pca_tune <- tune_imp(   obj,   .f = "pca_imp",   na_loc = na_loc,   parameters = data.frame(ncp = 10) ) #> Tuning `pca_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning  knn_tune <- tune_imp(   obj,   .f = "knn_imp",   na_loc = na_loc,   parameters = data.frame(k = 10) ) #> Tuning `knn_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning  rnorm_tune <- tune_imp(   obj,   .f = rnorm_imp,   na_loc = na_loc,   parameters = data.frame(mean = 0, sd = 1) # must match with arguments of `rnorm_imp` ) #> Tuning custom function #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning mean(compute_metrics(pca_tune, metrics = "rmse")$.estimate) #> [1] 0.2048011 mean(compute_metrics(knn_tune, metrics = "rmse")$.estimate) #> [1] 0.1962497 mean(compute_metrics(rnorm_tune, metrics = "rmse")$.estimate) #> [1] 1.110258

what: Group-Wise Imputation with Small-Group Padding and Group-Wise Parameters with group_imp()
title: slideimp
text: group_imp() allows imputation performed separately within defined groups (e.g., chromosome), significantly reduces run time can increase accuracy K-NN PCA imputation. features: required - list-column element character vector variable names imputed together. aux: optional - auxiliary variables include group. used augment imputation quality features imputed . one group small, e.g. chrM, aux used pad group randomly drawing features groups meet min_group_size. parameters: optional - group-specific imputation parameters. First simulate data 2 groups. create group3 1 feature show min_group_size pads using aux list column. generate group parameter group_imp() front. can see group3 padded 10 columns. group, also assign different number nearest neighbors demonstration. Finally, impute obj using modified group_imp_df. k = 10 passed group_imp() ignored since groups group-wise k specified.
code: sim_obj <- sim_mat(n = 20, p = 50, n_col_groups = 2)  # Matrix to be imputed obj <- sim_obj$input obj[1:5, 1:4] #>           feature1  feature2  feature3  feature4 #> sample1 0.03031619 0.1352710 0.4653579 0.1143298 #> sample2 0.50962966 0.2313022 0.9737881 0.5198844 #> sample3 0.69068459 0.5020337 0.7863144 0.9997625 #> sample4 0.40710170 0.4094603 0.3228294 0.2529592 #> sample5 0.73113251 0.4108405 0.6385945        NA  # Metadata, i.e., which features belong to which group meta <- sim_obj$col_group meta[1:5, ] #>    feature  group #> 1 feature1 group1 #> 2 feature2 group1 #> 3 feature3 group1 #> 4 feature4 group2 #> 5 feature5 group2  # We put feature 1 in `group3` meta[1, 2] <- "group3" meta[1:5, ] #>    feature  group #> 1 feature1 group3 #> 2 feature2 group1 #> 3 feature3 group1 #> 4 feature4 group2 #> 5 feature5 group2 set.seed(1234) group_imp_df <- prep_groups(colnames(obj), group = meta, min_group_size = 10) group_imp_df$parameters <- list(list(k = 3), list(k = 4), list(k = 5)) group_imp_df #> # slideimp table: 3 x 4 #>   group          feature             aux parameters #>  group1 <character [26]> <character [0]> <list [1]> #>  group2 <character [23]> <character [0]> <list [1]> #>  group3  <character [1]> <character [9]> <list [1]> knn_results <- group_imp(obj, group = group_imp_df, cores = 4, k = 10) #> Imputing 3 groups using KNN. #> Running mode: threaded (4 cores) print(knn_results, p = 4) #> Method: group_imp (KNN imputation) #> Dimensions: 20 x 50 #>  #>           feature1  feature2  feature3  feature4 #> sample1 0.03031619 0.1352710 0.4653579 0.1143298 #> sample2 0.50962966 0.2313022 0.9737881 0.5198844 #> sample3 0.69068459 0.5020337 0.7863144 0.9997625 #> sample4 0.40710170 0.4094603 0.3228294 0.2529592 #> sample5 0.73113251 0.4108405 0.6385945 0.4472338 #> sample6 0.48993078 0.4950764 0.6553896 0.6737998 #> # Showing 6 of 20 rows and 4 of 50 columns

previous_headings: Sliding Window Imputation for WGBS/EM-seq Data with slide_imp()
what: Select window_size, overlap_size, and PCA/K-NN Parameters
title: slideimp
text: Note: WGBS/EM-seq data grouped chromosome performing sliding window imputation. , simulate 1000 sites. Importantly, simulate location vector feature (genomic position) varying spacing 50 500 bp apart . locations vector contains genomic position feature (column). used determine columns grouped together given window size. First, convert data beta matrix features columns. , real dataset, tune hyperparameters using chr22. , demonstration, use whole data since size small. ncp (number principal components) 2 4, indicating performing sliding PCA imputation. Pass k sliding K-NN imputation. window_size 5,000 10,000 bp. overlap_size fixed 1,000 bp (affect results much real analyses). Finally, can use slide_imp() impute full beta_matrix. Use best parameter combination cross-validation metrics. start end window location vectors. window_n number features included window. Turn dry_run impute data
code: set.seed(1234) sample_names <- paste0("S", 1:10) n_sites <- 1000  # Simulate positions with 50–500 bp between each site distances_between <- sample(50:500, size = n_sites, replace = TRUE) locations <- cumsum(distances_between) # <- important, location vector  methyl <- data.frame(   chr = "chr1",   start = locations,   end = locations,   strand = "+" )  for (i in seq_along(sample_names)) {   methyl[[paste0("numCs", i)]] <- sample.int(100, size = n_sites, replace = TRUE)   methyl[[paste0("numTs", i)]] <- sample.int(100, size = n_sites, replace = TRUE)   methyl[[paste0("coverage", i)]] <- methyl[[paste0("numCs", i)]] + methyl[[paste0("numTs", i)]] }  methyl[1:5, 1:10] #>    chr start  end strand numCs1 numTs1 coverage1 numCs2 numTs2 coverage2 #> 1 chr1   333  333      +     83     68       151     84     36       120 #> 2 chr1   718  718      +     82      2        84     54     99       153 #> 3 chr1  1173 1173      +     48      2        50     44     16        60 #> 4 chr1  1323 1323      +     70     48       118     48     56       104 #> 5 chr1  1483 1483      +     88     15       103     83     19       102 numCs_matrix <- as.matrix(methyl[, paste0("numCs", seq_along(sample_names))]) cov_matrix <- as.matrix(methyl[, paste0("coverage", seq_along(sample_names))]) beta_matrix <- numCs_matrix / cov_matrix  colnames(beta_matrix) <- sample_names rownames(beta_matrix) <- methyl$start  beta_matrix <- t(beta_matrix) # Set 10% of the data to missing set.seed(1234) beta_matrix[sample.int(length(beta_matrix), floor(length(beta_matrix) * 0.1))] <- NA beta_matrix[1:4, 1:4] #>           333       718       1173      1323 #> S1 0.54966887 0.9761905 0.96000000 0.5932203 #> S2 0.70000000 0.3529412 0.73333333 0.4615385 #> S3 0.06521739 0.7053571         NA 0.6507937 #> S4 0.69444444 0.2846154 0.04347826 0.7636364 params <- expand.grid(ncp = c(2, 4), window_size = c(5000, 10000)) params$overlap_size <- 1000 params$min_window_n <- 20 # windows with less than 20 columns are dropped  # Increase n_reps from 2 in actual analyses and use another chromosome (i.e., chr22) tune_slide_pca <- tune_imp(   obj = beta_matrix,   parameters = params,   .f = "slide_imp",   n_reps = 2,   location = locations ) #> Tuning `slide_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> ℹ Using default `num_na` = 500 (~5% of cells). #>   Increase for more reliability or decrease if missing is dense. #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning  metrics <- compute_metrics(tune_slide_pca)  aggregate(.estimate ~ .metric + ncp + window_size, data = metrics, FUN = mean) #>   .metric ncp window_size .estimate #> 1     mae   2        5000 0.2257168 #> 2    rmse   2        5000 0.2838435 #> 3     mae   4        5000 0.2395280 #> 4    rmse   4        5000 0.2984013 #> 5     mae   2       10000 0.2123151 #> 6    rmse   2       10000 0.2642956 #> 7     mae   4       10000 0.2223801 #> 8    rmse   4       10000 0.2756162 slide_imp(   obj = beta_matrix,   location = locations,   window_size = 5000,   overlap_size = 1000,   ncp = 2,   min_window_n = 20,   dry_run = TRUE # <- dry_run to inspect the windows ) #> Dropping 43 window(s) with fewer than 20 columns. #> # slideimp table: 24 x 4 #>  start end window_n  subset_local #>     15  34       20 <double [20]> #>    104 124       21 <double [21]> #>    188 209       22 <double [22]> #>    205 225       21 <double [21]> #>    222 241       20 <double [20]> #>    239 258       20 <double [20]> #>    254 273       20 <double [20]> #>    369 388       20 <double [20]> #>    385 404       20 <double [20]> #>    402 424       23 <double [23]> #> # ... with 14 more rows slide_imp(   obj = beta_matrix,   location = locations,   window_size = 5000,   overlap_size = 1000,   ncp = 2,   min_window_n = 20,   dry_run = FALSE,   .progress = FALSE ) #> Method: slide_imp (PCA imputation) #> Dimensions: 10 x 1000 #> Note: requested columns still contain NA values #>  #>           333       718       1173      1323      1483       1925 #> S1 0.54966887 0.9761905 0.96000000 0.5932203        NA 0.08695652 #> S2 0.70000000 0.3529412 0.73333333 0.4615385 0.8137255 0.45454545 #> S3 0.06521739 0.7053571         NA 0.6507937 0.4393064 0.37735849 #> S4 0.69444444 0.2846154 0.04347826 0.7636364 0.9595960 0.54621849 #> S5 0.83505155 0.5777778 0.47517730        NA 0.7368421 0.21666667 #> S6 0.26612903 0.3451327 0.53072626 0.5000000 0.6465517 0.37288136 #> # Showing 6 of 10 rows and 6 of 1000 columns

what: Tips to speed up PCA imputation
title: Speeding up PCA imputation
text: pca_imp() PCA imputation workhorse used slide_imp(), tune_imp(), group_imp() whenever PCA imputation requested. article describes make PCA imputation faster choosing convergence threshold, PCA eigensolver, parallel backend mirai.

previous_headings: Tips to speed up PCA imputation
title: Speeding up PCA imputation
text: users can keep defaults: However, large genomic matrices, runtime can depend strongly two arguments: default threshold = 1e-6, matching conservative behavior missMDA. many genomic matrices, threshold = 1e-5 can much faster producing similar imputed values. solver = "auto" runs short timed probe solvers picks faster one. solver = "exact" forces exact solver. solver = "lobpcg" forces iterative LOBPCG solver.

previous_headings: Tips to speed up PCA imputation
title: Speeding up PCA imputation
text: pca_imp() supports two PCA eigensolvers: exact solver, selected solver = "exact". iterative LOBPCG solver, selected solver = "lobpcg". exact solver robust often fast small moderate matrices. iterative LOBPCG solver can faster large approximately low-rank genomic matrices, especially small number principal components needed. default solver = "auto" runs small number probe iterations solver start run, measures wall-clock time, picks LOBPCG clearly faster exact solver. decision data-dependent made per call. users, solver = "auto" good default. However, manual benchmarking still useful two situations: long tune_imp() run avoid cost repeated probing solver = "auto". suspect auto choice wrong (example, large low-rank matrices LOBPCG clearly win, small windows exact solver clearly win).

code: ncp_probe <- 5 exact <- system.time(invisible(   pca_imp(     obj = obj_probe,     ncp = ncp_probe,     solver = "exact",     threshold = 1e-5,     na_check = FALSE   ) ))  iterative <- system.time(invisible(   pca_imp(     obj = obj_probe,     ncp = ncp_probe,     solver = "lobpcg",     threshold = 1e-5,     na_check = FALSE   ) ))  exact - iterative #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.006  -0.016  -0.002 thresh_df <- data.frame(threshold = c(1e-5, 1e-6), ncp = ncp_probe) res <- tune_imp(obj = obj_probe, parameters = thresh_df, .f = "pca_imp") #> Tuning `pca_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> ℹ Using default `num_na` = 500 (~0.9% of cells). #>   Increase for more reliability or decrease if missing is dense. #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning  compute_metrics(res) #>   threshold ncp param_set rep_id error   n n_miss .metric .estimator  .estimate #> 1     1e-05   5         1      1  <NA> 500      0     mae   standard 0.09174255 #> 2     1e-05   5         1      1  <NA> 500      0    rmse   standard 0.11499555 #> 3     1e-06   5         2      1  <NA> 500      0     mae   standard 0.09174508 #> 4     1e-06   5         2      1  <NA> 500      0    rmse   standard 0.11500090

First choose speed settings representative subset, include fixed settings parameters data frame tuning window PCA parameters. slide_imp() typically operates small windows samples, "exact" solver usually better default. solver = "auto", slide_imp() probe first window force solver rest windows. Finally, use selected speed accuracy parameters imputing full dataset:
code: beta_matrix <- obj_probe # Treat `obj_probe` as a `slide_imp()` input locations <- seq_len(ncol(beta_matrix)) slide_params <- expand.grid(   ncp = c(2, 4),   coeff.ridge = c(0.8, 1, 1.2),   window_size = c(50, 500) # Adjust based on units of `locations` )  slide_params$overlap_size <- 5 slide_params$min_window_n <- 20 slide_params$solver <- "exact" # Usually better for `slide_imp()` slide_params$threshold <- chosen_threshold  tune_slide_pca <- tune_imp(   obj = beta_matrix,   .f = "slide_imp",   parameters = slide_params,   n_reps = 1, # <- increase to a higher number in real analyses   location = locations ) #> Tuning `slide_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> ℹ Using default `num_na` = 500 (~0.9% of cells). #>   Increase for more reliability or decrease if missing is dense. #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning  slide_metrics <- compute_metrics(tune_slide_pca, metrics = "rmse")  aggregate(   .estimate ~ ncp + coeff.ridge + window_size,   data = slide_metrics,   FUN = mean ) #>    ncp coeff.ridge window_size .estimate #> 1    2         0.8          50 0.1218555 #> 2    4         0.8          50 0.1257166 #> 3    2         1.0          50 0.1213016 #> 4    4         1.0          50 0.1242115 #> 5    2         1.2          50 0.1208738 #> 6    4         1.2          50 0.1228126 #> 7    2         0.8         500 0.1170125 #> 8    4         0.8         500 0.1185850 #> 9    2         1.0         500 0.1168271 #> 10   4         1.0         500 0.1179256 #> 11   2         1.2         500 0.1166733 #> 12   4         1.2         500 0.1173506 slide_results <- slide_imp(   obj = beta_matrix,   location = locations,   window_size = 500,   overlap_size = 5,   min_window_n = 20,   ncp = 2,   coeff.ridge = 1.2,   solver = "exact", # Usually better for `slide_imp()`   threshold = chosen_threshold,   .progress = FALSE )

what: Parallel PCA imputation and BLAS threading
title: Speeding up PCA imputation
text: PCA imputation can also sped running independent imputation tasks parallel tune_imp() group_imp(). PCA imputation, use mirai parallelization rather cores argument. PCA imputation run parallel, worker may call multithreaded linear algebra routines BLAS. worker uses multiple BLAS threads, total number active CPU threads can become much larger number physical cores. called thread oversubscription, can make code slower rather faster. example, 8 mirai workers using 8 BLAS threads can try use 64 CPU threads. avoid , set pin_blas = TRUE tune_imp() group_imp() running PCA imputation mirai. requires RhpcBLASctl: group-wise PCA imputation: pin_blas = TRUE uses RhpcBLASctl limit number BLAS threads inside parallel workers. works common BLAS libraries OpenBLAS MKL, typical Linux also available Windows replacing R’s default BLAS. may effect macOS, depending BLAS implementation.
code: library(mirai)  # Start 4 mirai workers. mirai::daemons(4)  # Run PCA tuning in parallel. tune_pca <- tune_imp(   obj = obj_probe,   .f = "pca_imp",   parameters = params,   n_reps = 1,   .progress = FALSE # <- Use `TRUE` to monitor longer-running jobs ) #> Tuning `pca_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> ℹ Using default `num_na` = 500 (~0.9% of cells). #>   Increase for more reliability or decrease if missing is dense. #> Running mode: mirai #> Step 2/2: Tuning #> Tip: set `pin_blas = TRUE` may improve parallel performance.  # Stop mirai workers when finished. mirai::daemons(0) mirai::daemons(4)  tune_slide_pca <- tune_imp(   obj = beta_matrix,   .f = "slide_imp",   parameters = slide_params,   n_reps = 1,   location = locations,   pin_blas = TRUE # <- Set to `TRUE` if R is linked to multi-threaded BLAS/LAPACK ) #> Tuning `slide_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> ℹ Using default `num_na` = 500 (~0.9% of cells). #>   Increase for more reliability or decrease if missing is dense. #> Running mode: mirai #> Step 2/2: Tuning  mirai::daemons(0) mirai::daemons(4)  pca_group_results <- group_imp(   obj = obj,   group = sim_df,   ncp = 2,   solver = chosen_solver,   threshold = chosen_threshold,   pin_blas = TRUE # <- Set to `TRUE` if R is linked to multi-threaded BLAS/LAPACK ) #> Imputing 2 groups using PCA. #> Running mode: mirai  mirai::daemons(0)

title: Speeding up PCA imputation
text: Start solver = "auto" unless PCA imputation slow run many calls, tune_imp(), per-call probe overhead can add . PCA imputation slow, benchmark solver = "exact" solver = "lobpcg" representative subset force faster solver. Try threshold = 1e-5 less stringent value, depending error tolerance. Keep threshold = 1e-5 produces similar imputed values similar cross-validation error. LOBPCG likely help matrix large, approximately low-rank, ncp small. slide_imp(), solver = "exact" usually right choice. Choose speed settings first, tune accuracy-related parameters ncp, coeff.ridge, window_size. running PCA imputation sequentially, BLAS multithreading may help, pin_blas unnecessary. running many PCA imputations parallel mirai, use pin_blas = TRUE. Avoid using many mirai workers many BLAS threads time. Benchmark representative subset, best setup depends hardware, BLAS library, matrix size, missing-data pattern.

title: Numeric Matrices K-NN and PCA Imputation
text: simulate DNA methylation (DNAm) microarray data 2 chromosomes. slideimp functions expect input numeric matrix variables stored columns. Hyperparameters tuned using tune_imp(). evaluate following options grid search: Number nearest neighbors (k): 5 20 Inverse distance power (dist_pow) weighted averaging: 1 2 (higher values assign lower weights distant neighbors) Tuning performed subset data. use 10 repeats (n_reps = 10) cross-validation evaluation. artificially mask 50 observed values (num_na = 50) compute RMSE MAE. Use larger values n_reps num_na real analyses reliable error estimates. Note: Parallelization via cores argument available K-NN imputation RcppThread. Calculate errors using compute_metrics() yardstick functions. PCA imputation custom functions, set parallelization mirai::daemons(). See Speeding PCA imputation additional PCA performance-tuning guidance. Note: machines multi-threaded BLAS, turn pin_blas = TRUE tuning PCA imputation parallel avoid thrashing. perform imputation group_imp() using best parameters. PCA imputation group_imp() can parallelized using mirai package, similar tune_imp(). pin_blas = TRUE can help PCA imputation multi-threaded BLAS machines. Alternatively, full matrix imputation can performed using knn_imp() pca_imp().
code: library(slideimp) set.seed(1234) sim_obj <- sim_mat(n = 20, p = 100, n_col_groups = 2)  # Extract the $input attribute obj <- sim_obj$input  # Extract metadata to map the features to groups group_df <- sim_obj$col_group knn_params <- expand.grid(k = c(5, 20), dist_pow = c(1, 2)) group2_columns <- subset(group_df, group == "group2") group2_only <- obj[, group2_columns$feature]  tune_knn <- tune_imp(   group2_only,   parameters = knn_params,   .f = "knn_imp",   cores = 8,   n_reps = 10,   num_na = 50 ) #> Tuning `knn_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> Running mode: threaded (8 cores) #> Step 2/2: Tuning metrics <- compute_metrics(tune_knn)  # equivalently: dplyr::summarize(metrics, ..., .by = c(k, dist_pow, .metric)) sum_metrics <- do.call(   data.frame,   aggregate(     .estimate ~ k + dist_pow + .metric,     data = metrics,     FUN = function(x) {       c(         n = length(x),         mean_error = mean(x),         sd_error = sd(x)       )     }   ) )  sum_metrics[order(sum_metrics$.estimate.mean_error), ] #>    k dist_pow .metric .estimate.n .estimate.mean_error .estimate.sd_error #> 2 20        1     mae          10            0.1501427         0.01599598 #> 4 20        2     mae          10            0.1519464         0.01638402 #> 1  5        1     mae          10            0.1656087         0.01683512 #> 3  5        2     mae          10            0.1669792         0.01666404 #> 6 20        1    rmse          10            0.1897722         0.01915653 #> 8 20        2    rmse          10            0.1918765         0.01936392 #> 5  5        1    rmse          10            0.2081833         0.01949624 #> 7  5        2    rmse          10            0.2101955         0.01884442 mirai::daemons(2) # 2 Cores  # PCA imputation. pca_params <- data.frame(ncp = c(1, 5)) # For machines with multi-threaded BLAS, turn on `pin_blas = TRUE` tune_pca <- tune_imp(obj, parameters = pca_params, .f = "pca_imp", n_reps = 10, num_na = 50)  mirai::daemons(0) # Close daemons knn_group_results <- group_imp(obj, group = group_df, k = 20, dist_pow = 1, cores = 2) #> Imputing 2 groups using KNN. #> Running mode: threaded (2 cores) knn_group_results #> Method: group_imp (KNN imputation) #> Dimensions: 20 x 100 #>  #>          feature1  feature2  feature3  feature4  feature5  feature6 #> sample1 0.3486482 0.7385414 0.4077444 0.1607935 0.3924661 0.2434143 #> sample2 0.5338935 0.4724364 0.9663621 0.4788070 0.5061132 0.3923603 #> sample3 0.7185848 0.7351035 0.6724479 0.3162537 0.7634236 1.0000000 #> sample4 0.1734418 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.2106358 #> sample5 0.5388440 0.5306182 0.5685354 0.5383513 0.4680080 0.8518388 #> sample6 0.3768380 0.5570723 0.8764909 0.5276245 0.6722794 0.5740639 #> # Showing 6 of 20 rows and 6 of 100 columns # Similar to `tune_imp`, parallelization is controlled by `mirai::daemons()` mirai::daemons(2) pca_group_results <- group_imp(obj, group = group_df, ncp = 10) mirai::daemons(0) full_knn_results <- knn_imp(obj = obj, k = 20) full_pca_results <- pca_imp(obj = obj, ncp = 10)

title: Numeric Matrices K-NN and PCA Imputation
text: slide_imp() performs sliding window imputation. function Illumina DNAm microarrays. See group_imp(). DNAm WGBS/EM-seq data grouped chromosomes (.e., run slide_imp() separately chromosome) imputation. See package vignette details. See vignette details selecting window_size overlap_size tune_imp(). slide_imp() parameters: location: required - sorted numeric vector length ncol(obj) specifying position column (e.g., genomic coordinates bp). window_size: required - width sliding window (unit location). overlap_size: optional - overlap width consecutive windows (units location). Must strictly less window_size. min_window_n: required - minimum number columns window must contain imputed. Windows fewer columns threshold dropped. Must greater k (K-NN) ncp (PCA). dry_run: optional - return just calculated windows examine windows included. k: required - (specifying K-NN imputation) number nearest neighbors use inside window. ncp: required - (specifying PCA imputation) number principal components retain. Use instead k performing sliding-window PCA imputation. subset: optional - impute just subset features (.e., just clock CpGs). flank: optional - build flanking windows window_size around features provided subset. First, let’s perform dry run examine windows imputed slide_imp. , can perform imputation using given parameters.

slideimp table: 45 x 4 #>  start end window_n  subset_local #>      1  50       50 <double [50]> #>     46  95       50 <double [50]> #>     91 140       50 <double [50]> #>    136 185       50 <double [50]> #>    181 230       50 <double [50]> #>    226 275       50 <double [50]> #>    271 320       50 <double [50]> #>    316 365       50 <double [50]> #>    361 410       50 <double [50]> #>    406 455       50 <double [50]> #> # title: Grouped K-NN or PCA Imputation — group_imp
text: obj numeric matrix samples rows features columns. group Specification features grouped imputation. Accepted formats : character scalar naming supported Illumina platform; see Note. long-format data.frame columns group feature. list-column data.frame feature list-column. Optional list-columns aux, auxiliary feature names, parameters, group-specific parameter lists. subset Optional character vector feature names impute. NULL, grouped features imputed. Features group subset demoted auxiliary columns group. Groups left zero features demotion dropped message. allow_unmapped Logical. FALSE, every column colnames(obj) must appear group. TRUE, columns group assignment left untouched used auxiliary columns. k Integer NULL. Number nearest neighbors K-NN imputation. NULL, k may supplied group$parameters. ncp Integer NULL. Number components PCA imputation. NULL, ncp may supplied group$parameters. method Character NULL. K-NN imputation, one "euclidean" "manhattan". PCA imputation, one "regularized" "EM". NULL, corresponding backend default used unless overridden group$parameters. cores Integer. Number cores K-NN imputation . PCA imputation, use mirai::daemons() parallelize across groups. .progress Logical. TRUE, show progress. min_group_size Integer NULL. Minimum total number columns per group, counting features auxiliary columns. Groups smaller padded randomly sampled columns obj. colmax Numeric scalar 0 1. Columns missing-data proportion greater colmax excluded main imputation method. Excluded columns left unchanged unless post_imp = TRUE, case remaining missing values replaced column means possible. post_imp Logical. TRUE, replace missing values remaining main imputation method column means possible. dist_pow Numeric. Power used penalize distant neighbors weighted average. dist_pow = 0 gives unweighted average nearest neighbors. tree Logical. FALSE, use brute-force K-NN. TRUE, use ball-tree K-NN via mlpack. scale Logical. TRUE, columns scaled unit variance. coeff.ridge Numeric. Ridge regularization coefficient. used method = "regularized". Values less 1 regularize less, moving closer EM PCA. Values greater 1 regularize , moving closer mean imputation. threshold Numeric. Convergence threshold. row.w Row weights, internally normalized sum 1. Can : NULL: rows weighted equally. numeric vector positive weights length nrow(obj). "n_miss": rows missing values receive lower weight. seed Integer, numeric, NULL. Random seed reproducibility. nb.init Integer. Number random initializations. first initialization always mean imputation. maxiter Integer. Maximum number iterations. miniter Integer. Minimum number iterations. solver Character. Eigensolver selection. One "auto", "exact", "lobpcg". "exact" uses exact solver. "lobpcg" uses iterative LOBPCG solver exact fallback. "auto" performs short timed probe chooses LOBPCG clearly faster exact solver. nb.init > 1, auto choice first PCA initialization reused subsequent PCA initializations. lobpcg_control list LOBPCG eigensolver control options, usually created lobpcg_control(). plain named list also accepted. Ignored solver = "exact". clamp Optional numeric vector length 2 giving lower upper bounds PCA-imputed values. Use NULL clamping. Use c(0, 1) DNA methylation beta values. Use c(lb, Inf) lower bound clamping, c(-Inf, ub) upper bound clamping. Clamping applied values imputed PCA step, observed values. pin_blas Logical. TRUE, pin BLAS threads 1 reduce contention using parallel PCA systems linked multithreaded BLAS. na_check Logical. TRUE, check whether returned matrix still contains missing values. on_infeasible Character. One "error", "skip", "mean". Controls behavior group infeasible imputation, example k ncp exceeds number usable columns applying colmax.

code: set.seed(1234) to_test <- sim_mat(10, 20, perc_total_na = 0.05, perc_col_na = 1) obj <- to_test$input group <- to_test$col_group head(group) #>    feature  group #> 1 feature1 group2 #> 2 feature2 group1 #> 3 feature3 group1 #> 4 feature4 group2 #> 5 feature5 group1 #> 6 feature6 group1  # Simple grouped K-NN imputation results <- group_imp(obj, group = group, k = 2, .progress = FALSE) #> Imputing 2 groups using KNN. #> Running mode: sequential results #> Method: group_imp (KNN imputation) #> Dimensions: 10 x 20 #>  #>          feature1  feature2  feature3  feature4   feature5  feature6 #> sample1 0.1568098 0.3213953 0.2768746 1.0000000 0.07839046 0.4219375 #> sample2 0.4098416 0.8658918 0.8221066 0.6396885 0.68926345 1.0000000 #> sample3 0.6801685 1.0000000 1.0000000 0.9953450 0.75246030 0.6958550 #> sample4 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.00000000 0.0000000 #> sample5 0.9639671 0.6409137 0.5111760 0.7184678 0.81815288 0.5883255 #> sample6 0.7031741 0.3731062 0.6782811 0.7542872 0.99910554 0.9070005 #> # Showing 6 of 10 rows and 6 of 20 columns  # Impute only a subset of features subset_features <- sample(to_test$col_group$feature, size = 10) knn_subset <- group_imp(   obj,   group = group,   subset = subset_features,   k = 2,   .progress = FALSE ) #> Imputing 2 groups using KNN. #> Running mode: sequential  # Use prep_groups() to inspect and edit per-group parameters prepped <- prep_groups(colnames(obj), group) prepped$parameters <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(prepped)), function(i) list(k = 2)) prepped$parameters[[2]]$k <- 4 knn_grouped <- group_imp(obj, group = prepped, .progress = FALSE) #> Imputing 2 groups using KNN. #> Running mode: sequential  if (FALSE) { # interactive() && requireNamespace("mirai", quietly = TRUE) # PCA imputation with mirai parallelism mirai::daemons(2) pca_grouped <- group_imp(obj, group = group, ncp = 2) mirai::daemons(0) pca_grouped }

title: Sliding-Window K-NN or PCA Imputation — slide_imp
text: sliding-window approach divides input matrix smaller segments based location values applies imputation window independently. Values overlapping regions averaged across windows produce final imputed result. Two windowing modes supported: flank = FALSE: greedily partition location windows width window_size requested overlap_size consecutive windows. flank = TRUE: create one window per feature subset, centered feature using supplied window_size. Specify k related arguments use knn_imp(), ncp related arguments use pca_imp().

title: Sliding-Window K-NN or PCA Imputation — slide_imp
text: pca_imp() relies heavily linear algebra. Windows, default BLAS shipped R may slow large matrices. Advanced users can replace OpenBLAS. PCA imputation speed depends eigensolver selected solver convergence threshold threshold. exact solver selected solver = "exact". iterative LOBPCG solver selected solver = "lobpcg". default, solver = "auto", performs short timed probe chooses LOBPCG clearly faster. large approximately low-rank genomic matrices, can useful benchmark solver = "exact" solver = "lobpcg" representative subset, chromosome 22, tuning accuracy-related parameters. slide_imp(), may include window_size overlap_size. default threshold = 1e-6 conservative. many genomic datasets, threshold = 1e-5 can faster giving similar imputed values. Check representative subset using relaxed threshold full analysis. See pkgdown article Speeding PCA imputation full workflow.

title: Tune Imputation Method Parameters — tune_imp
text: obj numeric matrix. parameters data.frame specifying parameter combinations tune. column parameter accepted .f, excluding obj. List-columns supported complex parameters. Duplicate rows removed. NULL treated single parameter set additional arguments, useful functions whose required arguments defaults. .f One "knn_imp", "pca_imp", "slide_imp", custom imputation function. na_loc Optional predefined missing-value locations. Accepted formats two-column integer matrix row column indices, numeric vector linear positions, list whose elements either formats. num_na Integer NULL. Total number missing values inject per repetition. supplied, n_cols derived num_na n_rows, missing values distributed evenly possible across columns. Ignored na_loc supplied. n_reps Integer. Number independent repetitions. n_cols Integer NULL. Number columns receive injected missing values per repetition. Must supplied num_na na_loc NULL, unless automatic default applies. Ignored num_na na_loc supplied. n_rows Integer. Target number missing values inject per selected column. Ignored na_loc supplied. rowmax Numeric scalar 0 1. Maximum allowed missing-data proportion per row injection. colmax Numeric scalar 0 1. Maximum allowed missing-data proportion per column injection. na_col_subset Optional integer character vector restricting columns eligible random missing-value injection. Ignored na_loc supplied. max_attempts Integer. Maximum number resampling attempts per repetition giving . .progress Logical. TRUE, show progress tuning. cores Integer. Number cores use K-NN sliding-window K-NN imputation. methods, use mirai::daemons(). location Numeric vector column locations. Required .f = "slide_imp". pin_blas Logical. TRUE, pin BLAS threads 1 parallel tuning reduce thread contention.

code: set.seed(123)  # Simulate some data obj <- sim_mat(10, 50)$input  # Tune K-NN imputation with random missing-value injection. # Use larger `num_na` and `n_reps` values for real analyses. params_knn <- data.frame(k = c(2, 4)) results <- tune_imp(   obj,   params_knn,   .f = "knn_imp",   n_reps = 1,   num_na = 10,   .progress = FALSE ) #> Tuning `knn_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning compute_metrics(results) #>   k .progress param_set rep_id error  n n_miss .metric .estimator .estimate #> 1 2     FALSE         1      1  <NA> 10      0     mae   standard 0.2075860 #> 2 2     FALSE         1      1  <NA> 10      0    rmse   standard 0.2841912 #> 3 4     FALSE         2      1  <NA> 10      0     mae   standard 0.1829599 #> 4 4     FALSE         2      1  <NA> 10      0    rmse   standard 0.2202581  # Tune with fixed missing-value positions na_positions <- list(   matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 1, 1, 1), ncol = 2),   matrix(c(2, 3, 4, 2, 2, 2), ncol = 2) )  results_fixed <- tune_imp(   obj,   data.frame(k = 2),   .f = "knn_imp",   na_loc = na_positions,   .progress = FALSE ) #> Tuning `knn_imp()` #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning  # Custom imputation function custom_fill <- function(obj, val = 0) {   obj[is.na(obj)] <- val   obj }  tune_imp(   obj,   data.frame(val = c(0, 1)),   .f = custom_fill,   num_na = 10,   .progress = FALSE ) #> Tuning custom function #> Step 1/2: Resolving NA locations #> Running mode: sequential #> Step 2/2: Tuning #> # slideimp table: 2 x 5 #>  val param_set rep_id        result error #>    0         1      1 <df [10 x 2]>  <NA> #>    1         2      1 <df [10 x 2]>  <NA>  if (FALSE) { # interactive() && requireNamespace("mirai", quietly = TRUE) # Parallel tuning with mirai mirai::daemons(2)  parameters_custom <- data.frame(mean = c(0, 1), sd = c(1, 1))  custom_imp <- function(obj, mean, sd) {   na_pos <- is.na(obj)   obj[na_pos] <- stats::rnorm(sum(na_pos), mean = mean, sd = sd)   obj }  results_p <- tune_imp(   obj,   parameters_custom,   .f = custom_imp,   n_reps = 1,   num_na = 10,   .progress = FALSE )  mirai::daemons(0) }

title: slideimp 1.1.0
text: knn_imp() longer caches pair-wise distances. use_cache argument removed.

title: slideimp 1.1.0
text: pca_imp() gained solver argument select LOBPCG eigensolver, configured via new lobpcg_control() helper. pca_imp() gained clamp argument bound imputed values, e.g. c(0, 1) methylation beta values. Also threaded slide_imp() group_imp().

title: slideimp 1.1.0
text: knn_imp() now uses RcppThread instead OpenMP, enabling parallelism macOS. tune_imp() now infers subset na_loc, speeding tuning knn_imp() slide_imp(). prep_groups() now S3 generic, replacing previous register--load pattern {slideimp.extra}. New article describing speed PCA imputation choosing solver threshold. Fixed numerical tolerance check failure CRAN’s ATLAS configuration.

title: slideimp 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-16

title: slideimp 1.0.0
text: group_imp() enforces stricter data validation. requested feature subset must subset object’s column names must subset mapping data.frame. Set allow_unmapped = TRUE bypass errors intersections incomplete. group_imp() tune_imp() now error arguments supplied apply chosen imputation method, rather silently ignoring . knn_imp() now uses logical tree argument toggle Ball tree (TRUE) brute force (FALSE). KD tree longer supported. knn_imp() pca_imp() gain early errors early exits. pca_imp() gains colmax post_imp arguments knn_imp(). prep_groups() (formerly group_features()) new name grouping function. now accepts column name vector instead full matrix. sample_na_loc() (formerly inject_na()) now exported. original remains accessible via slideimp:::inject_na() legacy code. sim_mat() now returns matrix sample--column format immediate compatibility package functions. perc_NA renamed perc_total_na, dimensions now specified via n (rows) p (columns). tune_imp() gains unified method argument applies pca_imp() knn_imp(), replacing pca_method knn_method. rep argument renamed n_reps. tune_imp() results v0.5.4 longer reproducible internal NA generation now uses sample_na_loc(). khanmiss1 dataset removed.

title: slideimp 1.0.0
text: compute_metrics() now supports data frames result list column containing truth estimate columns, similar yardstick. group_imp() prep_groups() automatically look Illumina manifests using register--load pattern {slideimp.extra}. knn_imp() gains max_cache control internal cache size (defaults 4GB). sim_mat() gains rho argument support compound symmetry correlation structures simulated matrices. sim_mat() tune_imp() gain dedicated print methods provide concise summaries instead dumping raw data console. slide_imp() gains location, flank, dry_run arguments fixed-window imputation, “flank mode” features surrounding subset, pre-computation inspection window statistics. tune_imp() gains granular control NA injection via n_cols, n_rows, num_na, na_col_subset. Pre-calculated locations can also passed na_loc compare methods using identical NA patterns.

title: slideimp 1.0.0
text: col_vars() mean_imp_col() overhauled use faster RcppArmadillo backend now support parallel computation OpenMP. Dependencies streamlined. tibble purrr removed hard dependencies, cli added informative messaging, carrier added explicit dependency. Documentation thoroughly overhauled numerous consistency improvements bug fixes. RhpcBLASctl added suggested package allow pinning BLAS cores avoid thrashing parallel runs. group_imp() tune_imp() prioritize process-level parallelization via mirai. knn_imp() supports OpenMP-controlled parallelization via cores argument mirai daemons active. knn_imp() pca_imp() use optimized internal Rcpp functions better performance.

title: slideimp 0.5.4
text: CRAN release: 2026-01-07 CRAN resubmission. group_features() added help creating group tibble needed group_imp(). pca_imp() now allows row.w = "n_miss" scale row weights number missing values per row.
